description: macOS sandbox-exec resolves all paths to canonical form before matching profile rules. Workspaces under $TMPDIR (/var/folders/...) silently fail because the kernel matches against /private/var/folders/.... Symptom: child aborts with SIGABRT (exit 134) and empty stderr; diagnostic only in ~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/.
resolution: Run realpathSync() on every path the profile binds (workspace, readOnlyMounts, toolchainPaths, sshAuthSock) in the dispatcher before rendering. Keep the seatbelt profile builder pure — canonicalization is a dispatcher concern. See src/provider/local/sandbox.ts canonicalizeProfilePaths().

description: macOS sandbox-exec aborts every spawned process with SIGABRT (exit 134, empty stderr) when the profile uses (deny default) without (allow file-read* (literal "/")). dyld's CacheFinder stats / during process bringup; the deny stops dyld before it can produce diagnostics.
resolution: Always include (allow file-read* (literal "/")) plus broad (allow file-read-metadata) in any deny-default profile. The latter also lets getcwd() traverse parents — without it /bin/sh fails with 'cannot access parent directories' on cwd resolution.

name: pure-profile-render-canonicalize-in-dispatcher
description: Keep sandbox profile rendering (buildBwrapArgv, buildSeatbeltProfile) as pure functions that take whatever paths they're given. Path canonicalization (realpathSync), tempfile management for the .sb, and process spawning belong in the dispatcher (runSandboxed). This keeps the profile builder unit-testable as deterministic string composition while the dispatcher owns the platform-specific I/O.

content: Bwrap profiles always pass --clearenv before --setenv lines so host env doesn't leak into the child. Allowed env is rebuilt from profile.envPassthrough + profile.setEnv + command.env in that order, with each layer overriding the previous. Same precedence applies on macOS where env reaches the child via Bun.spawn's env option (sandbox-exec doesn't filter env).

description: bw prompt failed with execvp() of 'claude' failed: No such file or directory because SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths was always empty and resolveSandboxEnv hardcoded PATH=/usr/bin:/bin — agents declared in burrow.toml never had a way to surface their host binary directory into the sandbox
resolution: Run installCheck() for every declared [[agents]] runtime at burrow up time, capture the resolved binary path on InstallCheckResult.path, expand each path into dirname + dirname(realpath) via expandToolchainBinDirs, and bake the result into SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths. resolveSandboxEnv prepends those entries to PATH with /usr/bin:/bin retained as the fallback tail. Existing burrows keep their stale empty toolchainPaths until destroyed and re-upped.

content: SandboxProfile.readOnlyMounts is the runtime-credential-forwarding lane (SPEC §17.4): burrow up populates it from each declared agent's AgentRuntime.credentialPaths(). Toolchain binary dirs go on toolchainPaths, NOT readOnlyMounts. Both bwrap and sandbox-exec render readOnlyMounts as 'allow read at the host path' so the agent reads them at the same path inside the sandbox; HOME-relative lookups still see the workspace, so a runtime that needs HOME/.claude/<file> ALSO has to materialize those files into the workspace at prepareWorkspace time (claude-code copies .credentials.json).

description: macOS sandbox-exec network-outbound regex rules against hostnames never match outbound TCP connections — once Bun/Node resolves DNS and connects to an IP, the address sandbox-exec sees is '1.2.3.4:443', not 'api.anthropic.com:443'. The current src/provider/local/seatbelt.ts renderNetworkRules() emits regex-on-hostname patterns for every allowed_domain, so network=restricted on darwin denies ALL outbound TCP regardless of allowlist. Confirmed empirically with sandbox-exec tests (current regex → curl exit 7; (remote tcp '*:443') → HTTP 405 success). The SPEC §25.3 userspace HTTP proxy is the proper fix; until then network=open is the only working policy on darwin.
resolution: Tracked in burrow-14b6. Workaround: use network=open in burrow.toml or pass --network open to bw up. Long-term: ship the userspace HTTP proxy (SPEC §25.3) — sandbox dials 127.0.0.1:<proxy-port>, proxy enforces the domain allowlist and resolves DNS. Same approach lands Linux restricted at the same time.

description: macOS sandbox-exec network-outbound regex-on-hostname rules silently deny ALL traffic — sandbox-exec matches at IP layer post-DNS, never sees the hostname. Linux bwrap restricted=no-network had the same effective gap (allowlist not enforced). The seatbelt grammar additionally rejects numeric IPs in (remote tcp ...) — host token must be 'localhost' or '*', so '127.0.0.1:NNN' raises a parse error.
resolution: Run a per-burrow loopback HTTP/HTTPS proxy (src/proxy/server.ts) inside the CLI process for each restricted run. Seatbelt only allows (remote tcp "localhost:<port>") to that endpoint; bwrap shares host net so the agent can reach loopback. Inject HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY (upper + lower) into command.env. The proxy enforces the domain allowlist and does DNS host-side, so the sandbox never needs mDNSResponder. Resolves burrow-14b6, supersedes mx-09e8d1.

content: sandbox-exec's (remote tcp ...) rule grammar only accepts 'localhost' or '*' as the host token. Numeric IPs raise 'host must be * or localhost in network address'. Use sbString(`localhost:${port}`) for the loopback proxy rule — 'localhost' covers both 127.0.0.1 and ::1 at the kernel match layer.

title: Userspace HTTP proxy for restricted-network enforcement (over IP-resolution and port-only options)
rationale: Three options were considered for fixing burrow-14b6: (a) resolve allowed_domains to IPs at bw up time and emit (remote ip ...) rules — brittle for CDN-fronted hosts whose IPs rotate (api.anthropic.com is on CloudFront-style); (b) emit (remote tcp "*:443/80") + DNS, no hostname filtering — honest but unenforced allowlist; (c) ship a per-run userspace HTTP CONNECT proxy that enforces the allowlist host-side. (c) was chosen because it's the only option that actually enforces the contract users are declaring. It also lands Linux restricted enforcement at the same time (bwrap shares host net, agent reaches loopback proxy). Tradeoff: Linux is honor-system until netns/nftables work lands (SPEC §25). The proxy is in-process — no separate daemon — so lifecycle binds to the run, not bw up.

title: seatbelt allows file-read* on /private/tmp + /private/var/folders and file-write* on /dev/null
rationale: Original profile granted file-write* on /private/tmp (and /private/var/folders) WITHOUT corresponding file-read* — so anything that round-tripped through /tmp wrote successfully but read back EPERM. Surfaced as a misleading 'another Claude Code process deleted it during startup cleanup' message from claude-code's Bash tool, which writes output to /tmp/claude-${uid}/.../task.output and reads it back. Diagnosed by reading the actual file contents from the host (which contained the real shell errors, not a missing-file scenario). Symmetric read+write is the right shape for any temp root the sandbox lets the agent write to. /dev/null was read-allowed (via SYSTEM_READ_SUBPATHS' /dev) but not write-allowed, breaking every shell redirect (zsh's '2>/dev/null'); narrow file-write* literal on /dev/null + /dev/dtracehelper (macOS Crash Reporter) is preferred over broadening /dev (would expose disk devices). Verified live: post-fix burrow can run echo/pwd successfully and round-trip bash output without EPERM (burrow-8452).

name: bun-globals-install-root-mount
description: When bun is a declared toolchain, burrow up adds <BUN_INSTALL>/install/global/node_modules to profile.toolchainPaths so bun-installed CLIs (ml, sd, cn, ov, sapling, greenhouse, etc.) can resolve symlink stubs to their .ts sources inside the sandbox. resolveBunGlobalInstallDir() in src/toolchain/paths.ts uses BUN_INSTALL with a $HOME/.bun fallback and verifies existence before returning. up.ts wires it via collectToolchainPaths only when a toolchain row has binary === 'bun'.

Globally-installed bun packages (ml, sd, cn, ov, sapling, greenhouse) failed inside the burrow with the misleading 'error loading current directory / CouldntReadCurrentDirectory' message. ~/.bun/bin/<name> stubs are bun-shebang symlinks pointing into ~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/<scope>/<pkg>/src/<file>.ts; the toolchain mount only covered ~/.bun/bin so bun's read of the realpath target was sandbox-denied. Symptom: shell builtins (echo, pwd) worked but every bun-shebang script forked from a sandboxed shell errored.
resolution: When bun is a declared toolchain, burrow up additionally mounts <BUN_INSTALL>/install/global/node_modules. resolveBunGlobalInstallDir() resolves BUN_INSTALL (with $HOME/.bun fallback) and verifies the install dir exists before adding it to profile.toolchainPaths. expandToolchainBinDirs intentionally stays generic — it does NOT walk arbitrary symlinks under a toolchain bin dir; bun-specific path knowledge lives in collectToolchainPaths.

name: generic toolchain bin-dir symlink walk
description: burrow up follows symlinks in each declared-toolchain bin dir and contributes either dirname(realpath) or the outermost node_modules ancestor (within trustedRoot=parent(binDir)). Catches bun-globals/uv-tool/pyenv/nvm/rustup/mise without per-tool knowledge. Walker is invoked only on declared-toolchain bin dirs, NOT agent bin dirs (agents are vetted by installCheck). Bin dir is realpath'd before computing trustedRoot (macOS /var/folders → /private/var/folders mismatch otherwise drops every hit).

content: [sandbox] read_only_paths in burrow.toml is the explicit escape hatch for paths the toolchain symlink walk misses (e.g. pyenv lib/, uv venv lib/). Tilde, '~/...', '$HOME', '${HOME}' prefixes expand against process.env.HOME (or os.homedir() fallback). Doctor fails the report on entries that aren't absolute after expansion or don't exist on the host — bwrap's --ro-bind (no -try) errors at sandbox spawn for missing paths so silently dropping would just defer to a confusing run-time error.

title: macOS sandbox is best-effort, Linux is canonical
rationale: End goal is cloud-deployed Linux container swarms. Synthesizing bwrap parity on macOS via DYLD/wrapper tricks is a tar pit and one-OS-update-from-regression. macOS gets ergonomic permission-fence; security boundary lives on Linux.

name: bwrap-in-docker recipe (Ubuntu 24.04 hosts)
description: Minimum non-privileged docker run flags to make bwrap work inside a container on Ubuntu 24.04 (kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=1 default): --security-opt apparmor=unconfined + --security-opt seccomp=unconfined + --security-opt systempaths=unconfined + --cap-add SYS_ADMIN. Failure modes: without apparmor=unconfined → 'Creating new namespace failed: Operation not permitted' (host AppArmor blocks unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER)); without SYS_ADMIN → 'Failed RTM_NEWADDR' on loopback in --unshare-all (no cap to bring up netns); without systempaths=unconfined → "Can't mount proc on /newroot/proc" (Docker masked paths). --privileged is the fallback that bypasses all four. Verified empirically; see burrow-1c19 for the local dev container application.

name: linux-dev-container-bwrap-recipe
description: Running bwrap inside Docker on Ubuntu 24.04 hosts requires four non-default flags: security_opt apparmor=unconfined (Ubuntu 24.04 ships kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=1, blocking unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER)), security_opt seccomp=unconfined (Docker's default seccomp blocks clone3 shapes bwrap needs), security_opt systempaths=unconfined (unmasks /proc so bwrap can mount its own /proc → otherwise 'Can't mount proc on /newroot/proc'), and cap_add SYS_ADMIN (lets bwrap bring up loopback in the new netns → otherwise 'Failed RTM_NEWADDR'). --privileged works as a fallback but is overkill. Implementation lives at .devcontainer/{Dockerfile,compose.yml,devcontainer.json}; rationale + table is in README.md '## Linux dev container' section. Verified on Docker 28.4 / Ubuntu 24.04.

name: HttpBurrowUpInput.agents wire-through for orchestrators
description: HttpBurrowUpInput.agents (string[] of runtime ids) lets remote callers (warren, future orchestrators) enable built-in [[agents]] patch rows at up-time without writing a burrow.toml into the project clone. resolveEffectiveAgents() in up.ts merges options.agents with burrow.toml [[agents]] (existing config rows win on id overlap so forwardCredentials=false isn't shadowed); the merged list feeds both collectToolchainPaths and collectCredentialPaths. Without this, a burrow spawned via HTTP into a project with no burrow.toml gets toolchainPaths=[] and the sandbox fails 'execvp <bin>'. Warren-side companion record lives in warren's mulch (forwards agent.name).

content: Effective [[agents]] list for up.ts is computed once via resolveEffectiveAgents(burrowToml, options.agents) and threaded into both collectToolchainPaths and collectCredentialPaths. Don't read burrowToml.agents directly inside those collectors anymore — that path silently bypasses HTTP-forwarded agent ids.

buildBwrapArgv emits --uid/--gid (default 1000/1000 via DEFAULT_SANDBOX_UID/_GID; override via SandboxProfile.runAsUid/runAsGid) right after --unshare-all so the sandboxed pid 1 always sees a non-root uid inside the userns. Without this the userns inherits the caller's uid mapping; on a root-running host (e.g. warren's Docker container without USER) the agent sees getuid()==0 and tools like claude-code with --dangerously-skip-permissions refuse to start.

Linux env channel into the sandbox is bwrap's process env (Bun.spawn { env }), not argv --setenv. buildBwrapArgv emits no --setenv/--clearenv lines. Secrets on argv leak via /proc/<pid>/cmdline (burrow-ab95). spawnLinux in src/provider/local/sandbox.ts calls resolveSandboxEnv and passes the result as Bun.spawn { env }; bwrap inherits and execve()s the child with it, so secrets land in /proc/<pid>/environ (mode 400) only. Bun.spawn's env *replaces* process.env (not merges), so omitting --clearenv is safe — bwrap's process env IS the resolved env. macOS spawnDarwin already used this channel via sandbox-exec; Linux is now symmetric. bwrap.test.ts asserts argv contains neither --setenv nor any secret value (regression guard).

name: worktree-gitdir-bind-mount
description: Worktree-backed workspaces need an explicit bind of <hostClonePath>/.git at the same path inside the sandbox: git worktree add writes the worktree's .git *file* with an absolute gitdir: pointer at <hostClone>/.git/worktrees/<id>, which the /workspace bind doesn't cover. SandboxProfile.workspaceGitdir carries the host path; bwrap emits --bind <gitdir> <gitdir> (rw — git writes per-worktree HEAD/index plus new objects to the shared object database) and seatbelt emits the matching subpath read+write rule. MaterializedWorkspaceSource.gitCommonDir feeds it; up.ts and fork.ts lift it onto the profile.
